```{r gpt2}
message("\nDone. Output folder: ", normalizePath(OUT_DIR, winslash = "/"))
```

## Item Response Theory

```{r unidimentionality, include=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(lavaan)
library(purrr)
library(tibble)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Define item sets for the four EPQ-RS scales
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_scale_items <- list(
  Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP14","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP2","EYP6","EYP18","EYP26","EYP28","EYP35","EYP43"),
  Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19","EYP23","EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48","EYP27","EYP41"),
  Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17","EYP21","EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38","EYP42","EYP46"),
  Lie          = c("EYP4","EYP16","EYP45","EYP8","EYP12","EYP20","EYP24","EYP29","EYP33","EYP37","EYP40","EYP47")
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Function to fit 1-factor CFA for a scale
# ------------------------------------------------------------
fit_epq_unidim_cfa <- function(data, items, factor_name) {
  
  dat <- epq_items %>%
    select(all_of(items)) %>%
    mutate(across(everything(), as.ordered))
  
  # Drop rows with all missing on that scale
  dat <- dat[rowSums(!is.na(dat)) > 0, , drop = FALSE]
  
  # 1-factor model
  model_syntax <- paste0(
    factor_name, " =~ ", paste(items, collapse = " + ")
  )
  
  fit <- lavaan::cfa(
    model = model_syntax,
    data = dat,
    ordered = items,
    estimator = "WLSMV",
    parameterization = "theta",
    std.lv = TRUE,
    missing = "pairwise"
  )
  
  fit
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Fit all four 1-factor CFAs
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_cfa_1f <- imap(epq_scale_items, ~ fit_epq_unidim_cfa(
  data = epq_items,
  items = .x,
  factor_name = .y
))

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Extract fit indices
# ------------------------------------------------------------


fmt2 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%.2f", x))

fmt3_apa <- function(x) {
  ifelse(
    is.na(x),
    "",
    sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", x))
  )
}

fmt_p_apa <- function(p) {
  case_when(
    is.na(p)   ~ "",
    p < .001   ~ "***",
    p < .01    ~ "**",
    p < .05    ~ "*",
    TRUE       ~ ""
  )
}

fmt_chisq_df_apa <- function(chisq, df, p) {
  paste0(
    fmt2(chisq),
    " (",
    ifelse(is.na(df), "", as.integer(round(df))),
    ")",
    fmt_p_apa(p)
  )
}




epq_cfa_fit_table <- imap_dfr(epq_cfa_1f, function(fit, scale_name) {
  tibble(
    Scale            = scale_name,
    chisq.scaled     = fitMeasures(fit, "chisq.scaled"),
    df.scaled        = fitMeasures(fit, "df.scaled"),
    p.scaled         = fitMeasures(fit, "pvalue.scaled"),
    cfi.scaled       = fitMeasures(fit, "cfi.scaled"),
    tli.scaled       = fitMeasures(fit, "tli.scaled"),
    rmsea.scaled     = fitMeasures(fit, "rmsea.scaled"),
    rmsea.ci.lower   = fitMeasures(fit, "rmsea.ci.lower.scaled"),
    rmsea.ci.upper   = fitMeasures(fit, "rmsea.ci.upper.scaled"),
    srmr             = fitMeasures(fit, "srmr")
  )
}) %>%
  mutate(
    `χ² (df)`      = fmt_chisq_df_apa(chisq.scaled, df.scaled, p.scaled),
    `RMSEA 90% CI` = paste0(fmt3_apa(rmsea.ci.lower), "-", fmt3_apa(rmsea.ci.upper))
  ) %>%
  select(
    Scale,
    `χ² (df)`,
    cfi.scaled,
    tli.scaled,
    rmsea.scaled,
    `RMSEA 90% CI`,
    srmr
  )

epq_cfa_fit_table

# Psychoticism needs modification
mi_psy_clean <- modindices(epq_cfa_1f$Psychoticism, sort. = TRUE) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  arrange(desc(mi)) %>%
  select(lhs, op, rhs, mi, epc, sepc.lv, sepc.all)

mi_psy_clean


# Refiting psychotocism
psychoticism_mod_1 <- '
Psychoticism =~ EYP10 + EYP14 + EYP22 + EYP31 + EYP39 + EYP2 +
                 EYP6 + EYP18 + EYP26 + EYP28 + EYP35 + EYP43

EYP26 ~~ EYP43
EYP2 ~~ EYP28
EYP28 ~~EYP35
EYP2 ~~ EYP18 
EYP2 ~~  EYP6
EYP14 ~~ EYP28
EYP22 ~~ EYP28
EYP14 ~~ EYP43
'

fit_psychoticism_mod_1 <- cfa(
  psychoticism_mod_1,
  data = epq_items,
  ordered = c("EYP10","EYP14","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP2",
              "EYP6","EYP18","EYP26","EYP28","EYP35","EYP43"),
  estimator = "WLSMV",
  parameterization = "theta",
  std.lv = TRUE,
  missing = "pairwise"
)

summary(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE)
modindices(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, sort. = TRUE)



# Updating the model
psychoticism_mod_row <- tibble(
  Scale          = "Psychoticism (modified)",
  chisq.scaled   = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "chisq.scaled"),
  df.scaled      = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "df.scaled"),
  p.scaled       = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "pvalue.scaled"),
  cfi.scaled     = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "cfi.scaled"),
  tli.scaled     = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "tli.scaled"),
  rmsea.scaled   = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "rmsea.scaled"),
  rmsea.ci.lower = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "rmsea.ci.lower.scaled"),
  rmsea.ci.upper = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "rmsea.ci.upper.scaled"),
  srmr           = fitMeasures(fit_psychoticism_mod_1, "srmr")
) %>%
  mutate(
    `χ² (df)`      = fmt_chisq_df_apa(chisq.scaled, df.scaled, p.scaled),
    `RMSEA 90% CI` = paste0(fmt3_apa(rmsea.ci.lower), "-", fmt3_apa(rmsea.ci.upper))
  ) %>%
  select(
    Scale,
    `χ² (df)`,
    cfi.scaled,
    tli.scaled,
    rmsea.scaled,
    `RMSEA 90% CI`,
    srmr
  )

epq_cfa_fit_table_updated <- bind_rows(
  epq_cfa_fit_table,
  psychoticism_mod_row
) %>%
  mutate(
    Scale = factor(
      Scale,
      levels = c(
        "Extraversion",
        "Neuroticism",
        "Lie",
        "Psychoticism",
        "Psychoticism (modified)"
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(Scale) %>%
  mutate(Scale = as.character(Scale))

epq_cfa_fit_table_updated
```

```{r}
papaja::apa_table(
  epq_cfa_fit_table_updated,
  caption = "Scaled fit indices for one-factor CFA models of the EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "Values in the chi-square column are scaled chi-square statistics. RMSEA CI = 90% confidence interval for RMSEA. * p < .05. ** p < .01. *** p < .001.",
  align = c("l", "l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  escape = FALSE
)
```

```{r epq-irt, include=FALSE}
epq_irt_pars <- list()
epq_irt_tif  <- list()
epq_irt_fit  <- list()
epq_irt_rel  <- list()
epq_irt_conv <- list()
epq_irt_disc <- list()
epq_irt_m2   <- list()
epq_models    <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_item_sets)) {
  dat <- epq_items[, epq_item_sets[[sc]], drop = FALSE]
  
  mod <- mirt::mirt(
    dat,
    1,
    itemtype = "2PL",
    method = "MHRM",
    verbose = FALSE
  )
  
  epq_models[[sc]] <- mod
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # Model convergence confirmation
  # --------------------------------------------------
  epq_irt_conv[[sc]] <- tibble::tibble(
    Scale              = sc,
    converged          = mirt::extract.mirt(mod, "converged"),
    iterations         = mirt::extract.mirt(mod, "iterations"),
    second_order_test  = mirt::extract.mirt(mod, "secondordertest"),
    sem_info_converged = mirt::extract.mirt(mod, "SEMconv"),
    logLik             = mirt::extract.mirt(mod, "logLik")
  )
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # M2 model fit
  # --------------------------------------------------
  epq_irt_m2[[sc]] <- tryCatch({
    m2_out <- as.data.frame(
      mirt::M2(mod, type = "M2", calcNull = TRUE, CI = 0.90)
    )
    
    # standardize possible column names just in case
    if ("M2*" %in% names(m2_out)) names(m2_out)[names(m2_out) == "M2*"] <- "M2"
    if ("C2"  %in% names(m2_out)) names(m2_out)[names(m2_out) == "C2"]  <- "M2"
    
    tibble::as_tibble(m2_out) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Scale = sc, .before = 1)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    tibble::tibble(
      Scale    = sc,
      M2       = NA_real_,
      df       = NA_real_,
      p        = NA_real_,
      RMSEA    = NA_real_,
      RMSEA_5  = NA_real_,
      RMSEA_95 = NA_real_,
      SRMSR    = NA_real_,
      TLI      = NA_real_,
      CFI      = NA_real_,
      M2_error = conditionMessage(e)
    )
  })
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # Item parameters
  # --------------------------------------------------
  epq_irt_pars[[sc]] <- extract_mirt_pars(mod, sc)
  epq_irt_tif[[sc]]  <- make_tif_summary(mod, sc)
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # Item fit
  # --------------------------------------------------
  epq_irt_fit[[sc]] <- tryCatch({
    sx2 <- mirt::itemfit(mod, fit_stats = "S_X2") |>
      as.data.frame() |>
      tibble::rownames_to_column("Item")
    
    inout <- mirt::itemfit(mod, fit_stats = "infit") |>
      as.data.frame() |>
      tibble::rownames_to_column("Item")
    
    dplyr::left_join(sx2, inout, by = "Item") |>
      dplyr::mutate(
        Scale = sc,
        infit_flag  = ifelse(infit < 0.5 | infit > 1.5, "misfit", ""),
        outfit_flag = ifelse(outfit < 0.5 | outfit > 1.5, "misfit", ""),
        .before = 1
      )
  },
  error = function(e) tibble::tibble(
    Scale = sc, Item = names(dat),
    S_X2 = NA_real_, df = NA_real_, p = NA_real_,
    infit = NA_real_, outfit = NA_real_,
    infit_flag = NA_character_, outfit_flag = NA_character_
  ))
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # Marginal and empirical reliability
  # --------------------------------------------------
  marginal <- mirt::marginal_rxx(mod)
  
  theta_se <- mirt::fscores(mod, full.scores.SE = TRUE)
  se_vals  <- theta_se[, 2]
  emp_rel  <- 1 - mean(se_vals^2, na.rm = TRUE) / var(theta_se[, 1], na.rm = TRUE)
  
  epq_irt_rel[[sc]] <- tibble::tibble(
    Scale                 = sc,
    Marginal_Reliability  = round(marginal, 3),
    Empirical_Reliability = round(emp_rel, 3)
  )
  
  # --------------------------------------------------
  # Discrimination categorization
  # --------------------------------------------------
  item_coef <- as.data.frame(mirt::coef(mod, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)$items) |>
    tibble::rownames_to_column("Item") |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      Scale = sc,
      Item,
      a,
      Discrimination_categorization = dplyr::case_when(
        is.na(a) ~ NA_character_,
        a < 0    ~ "Negative (check keying/model)",
        a == 0   ~ "None",
        a < 0.35 ~ "Very Low",
        a < 0.65 ~ "Low",
        a < 1.35 ~ "Moderate",
        a < 1.70 ~ "High",
        TRUE     ~ "Very High"
      )
    )
  
  epq_irt_disc[[sc]] <- item_coef
}

# --------------------------------------------------
# Bind all outputs
# --------------------------------------------------
epq_irt_pars_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_pars)
epq_irt_tif_tbl  <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_tif)
epq_irt_fit_tbl  <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_fit)
epq_irt_rel_tbl  <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_rel)
epq_irt_conv_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_conv)
epq_irt_disc_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_disc)
epq_irt_m2_tbl   <- dplyr::bind_rows(epq_irt_m2)

# Add discrimination category into parameter table
epq_irt_pars_tbl <- epq_irt_pars_tbl |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    epq_irt_disc_tbl |>
      dplyr::select(Scale, Item, Discrimination_categorization),
    by = c("Scale", "Item")
  )

# Summary counts by scale
epq_irt_disc_summary <- epq_irt_disc_tbl |>
  dplyr::count(Scale, Discrimination_categorization, name = "n")

# Save files
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_conv_tbl, "outputs/tables/EPQ_IRT_convergence.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_rel_tbl,  "outputs/tables/EPQ_IRT_reliability.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_disc_tbl, "outputs/tables/EPQ_IRT_discrimination_by_item.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_disc_summary, "outputs/tables/EPQ_IRT_discrimination_summary.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_pars_tbl, "outputs/tables/epq_irt_pars_tbl.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_fit_tbl,  "outputs/tables/epq_irt_fit_tbl.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_irt_m2_tbl,   "outputs/tables/EPQ_IRT_M2_fit.csv")
```

```{r IRTPlots, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# EPQ-R IRT Plots: TIC, IIC, ICC for all four subscales
# ============================================================



# --------------------------------------------------
# APA theme
# --------------------------------------------------
apatheme <- theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border     = element_rect(colour = "black"),
    axis.title       = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  )

# --------------------------------------------------
# Subscale-specific colour palettes
# --------------------------------------------------
scale_fills <- c(
  Psychoticism = "#E64B35",
  Extraversion = "#4DBBD5",
  Neuroticism  = "#00A087",
  Lie          = "#F39B7F"
)

scale_colors <- c(
  Psychoticism = "#E64B35",
  Extraversion = "#4DBBD5",
  Neuroticism  = "#00A087",
  Lie          = "#F39B7F"
)

# item-level palettes (12 items each) using ggsci
item_palettes <- list(
  Psychoticism = colorRampPalette(c("#E64B35", "#B03021", "#FFB4A2"))(12),
  Extraversion = colorRampPalette(c("#4DBBD5", "#2980B9", "#AED6F1"))(12),
  Neuroticism  = colorRampPalette(c("#00A087", "#006D5B", "#A3D9CC"))(12),
  Lie          = colorRampPalette(c("#F39B7F", "#C0392B", "#FAD7A0"))(12)
)

theta_range <- seq(-4, 4, by = 0.01)


# ==========================================================
# 1. TEST INFORMATION CURVES (all four on one panel)
# ==========================================================
tic_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(epq_models), function(sc) {
  info <- testinfo(epq_models[[sc]], Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1))
  se   <- 1 / sqrt(info)
  data.frame(
    Theta       = theta_range,
    Information = as.numeric(info),
    SE          = as.numeric(se),
    Scale       = sc
  )
}))

tic_plot <- ggplot(tic_data, aes(x = Theta, y = Information, fill = Scale)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.35, position = "identity") +
  geom_line(aes(colour = Scale), linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = scale_fills) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = scale_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Test Information Curves: EPQ-RS-B Subscales",
    x     = expression(theta),
    y     = expression(I(theta))
  ) +
  apatheme +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_TIC_all_scales.png",
       tic_plot, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

# Optional: TIC with SE on secondary axis (per scale, saved individually)
for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  df_sc <- tic_data[tic_data$Scale == sc, ]
  se_scale <- max(df_sc$Information) / max(df_sc$SE)
  
  p <- ggplot(df_sc, aes(x = Theta)) +
    geom_area(aes(y = Information), fill = scale_fills[[sc]], alpha = 0.35) +
    geom_line(aes(y = Information), colour = scale_colors[[sc]], linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_line(aes(y = SE * se_scale), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
    scale_y_continuous(
      name = expression(I(theta)),
      sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / se_scale, name = expression(SE(theta)))
    ) +
    labs(title = paste0("TIC + SE: ", sc), x = expression(theta)) +
    apatheme
  
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_TIC_SE_", sc, ".png"),
         p, width = 7, height = 4.5, dpi = 300)
}

# ==========================================================
# 2. ITEM INFORMATION CURVES (12 items per subscale panel)
# ==========================================================
iic_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  mod  <- epq_models[[sc]]
  nitems <- ncol(mod@Data$data)
  item_names <- colnames(mod@Data$data)
  
  iic_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nitems, function(i) {
    info <- iteminfo(extract.item(mod, i), Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1))
    data.frame(
      Theta       = theta_range,
      Information = as.numeric(info),
      Item        = item_names[i]
    )
  }))
  iic_df$Item <- factor(iic_df$Item, levels = item_names)
  
  iic_plots[[sc]] <- ggplot(iic_df, aes(x = Theta, y = Information)) +
    geom_area(fill = scale_fills[[sc]], alpha = 0.30) +
    geom_line(colour = scale_colors[[sc]], linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.20, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    facet_wrap(~ Item, ncol = 4) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Item Information Curves: ", sc),
      x     = expression(theta),
      y     = expression(I(theta))
    ) +
    apatheme +
    theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
  
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_IIC_", sc, ".png"),
         iic_plots[[sc]], width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)
}

# ==========================================================
# 3. ITEM CHARACTERISTIC CURVES / ICC (trace plots, 12 per subscale)
# ==========================================================
icc_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  mod  <- epq_models[[sc]]
  nitems <- ncol(mod@Data$data)
  item_names <- colnames(mod@Data$data)
  
  icc_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nitems, function(i) {
    extr <- extract.item(mod, i)
    probs <- probtrace(extr, Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1))
    # For 2PL: column 2 = P(X=1)
    data.frame(
      Theta = theta_range,
      P     = probs[, 2],
      Item  = item_names[i]
    )
  }))
  icc_df$Item <- factor(icc_df$Item, levels = item_names)
  
  icc_plots[[sc]] <- ggplot(icc_df, aes(x = Theta, y = P)) +
    geom_line(colour = scale_colors[[sc]], linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    facet_wrap(~ Item, ncol = 4) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.25)) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Item Characteristic Curves: ", sc),
      x     = expression(theta),
      y     = expression(P(theta))
    ) +
    apatheme +
    theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
  
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_ICC_", sc, ".png"),
         icc_plots[[sc]], width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)
}

# ==========================================================
# 4. MARGINAL RELIABILITY CURVE (per subscale)
# ==========================================================
rel_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  mod <- epq_models[[sc]]
  info <- testinfo(mod, Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1))
  rel  <- as.numeric(info) / (1 + as.numeric(info))  # marginal reliability at each theta
  
  rel_df <- data.frame(Theta = theta_range, Reliability = rel, Scale = sc)
  
  rel_plots[[sc]] <- ggplot(rel_df, aes(x = Theta, y = Reliability)) +
    geom_area(fill = scale_fills[[sc]], alpha = 0.3) +
    geom_line(colour = scale_colors[[sc]], linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.70, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
    annotate("text", x = 3.2, y = 0.72, label = ".70", size = 3, colour = "red") +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Marginal Reliability: ", sc),
      x     = expression(theta),
      y     = expression(rho(theta))
    ) +
    apatheme
  
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_MargRel_", sc, ".png"),
         rel_plots[[sc]], width = 7, height = 4.5, dpi = 300)
}

# Combined marginal reliability
rel_all <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(epq_models), function(sc) {
  info <- testinfo(epq_models[[sc]], Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1))
  data.frame(Theta = theta_range, Reliability = as.numeric(info) / (1 + as.numeric(info)), Scale = sc)
}))

rel_combined <- ggplot(rel_all, aes(x = Theta, y = Reliability, colour = Scale)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.70, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = scale_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  labs(
    title = "Marginal Reliability Across Theta: EPQ-RS-B",
    x = expression(theta), y = expression(rho(theta))
  ) +
  apatheme + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_MargRel_combined.png",
       rel_combined, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

# ==========================================================
# 5. EMPIRICAL RELIABILITY DENSITY (SE distribution)
# ==========================================================
emp_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  mod <- epq_models[[sc]]
  fs  <- fscores(mod, full.scores.SE = TRUE)
  
  emp_df <- data.frame(
    Theta = fs[, 1],
    SE    = fs[, 2],
    Scale = sc
  )
  
  emp_plots[[sc]] <- ggplot(emp_df, aes(x = Theta, y = SE)) +
    geom_point(colour = scale_colors[[sc]], alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8) +
    geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "black", linewidth = 0.6, se = FALSE) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = mean(emp_df$SE), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Empirical SE by Theta: ", sc),
      x     = expression(hat(theta)),
      y     = expression(SE(hat(theta)))
    ) +
    apatheme
  
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_EmpSE_", sc, ".png"),
         emp_plots[[sc]], width = 7, height = 4.5, dpi = 300)
}

# ==========================================================
# 6. COWPLOT COMPOSITES
# ==========================================================

# 6a. All four TIC + SE panels
tic_composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  plotlist = lapply(names(epq_models), function(sc) {
    df_sc <- tic_data[tic_data$Scale == sc, ]
    se_scale <- max(df_sc$Information) / max(df_sc$SE)
    ggplot(df_sc, aes(x = Theta)) +
      geom_area(aes(y = Information), fill = scale_fills[[sc]], alpha = 0.35) +
      geom_line(aes(y = Information), colour = scale_colors[[sc]], linewidth = 0.7) +
      geom_line(aes(y = SE * se_scale), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.5) +
      scale_y_continuous(
        name = expression(I(theta)),
        sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . / se_scale, name = expression(SE(theta)))
      ) +
      labs(title = sc, x = expression(theta)) +
      apatheme + theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 10))
  }),
  ncol = 2, labels = "AUTO"
)
ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_TIC_composite.png",
       tic_composite, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)

# 6b. All IIC panels (one per subscale, stitched)
iic_composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  plotlist = iic_plots, ncol = 1, labels = "AUTO"
)
ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_IIC_composite.png",
       iic_composite, width = 10, height = 30, dpi = 300)

# 6c. All ICC panels stitched
icc_composite <- plot_grid(
  plotlist = icc_plots, ncol = 1, labels = "AUTO"
)
ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_ICC_composite.png",
       icc_composite, width = 10, height = 30, dpi = 300)

# 6d. Marginal + Empirical SE side by side per subscale
for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  combo <- plot_grid(rel_plots[[sc]], emp_plots[[sc]], ncol = 2, labels = "AUTO")
  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_RelSE_combo_", sc, ".png"),
         combo, width = 12, height = 5, dpi = 300)
}

cat("All EPQ IRT plots saved to outputs/plots/\n")



# ==========================================================
# 7. PERSON-FIT PLOTS (Zh histograms, all four subscales)
# ==========================================================

epq_pf_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  pf <- mirt::personfit(epq_models[[sc]])
  
  misfits <- subset(pf, Zh < -2)
  cat(sc, ": ", nrow(misfits), " misfitting persons (Zh < -2)\n", sep = "")
  
  epq_pf_plots[[sc]] <- ggplot(pf, aes(x = Zh)) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5, col = I("black"), fill = scale_fills[[sc]],
                   alpha = 0.6) +
    ylim(0, 200) + xlim(-4, 2) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = -2) +
    labs(y = "Number of Participants",
         x = paste0("Zh Statistics: ", sc)) +
    apatheme
}

epq_personfit_plot <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  plotlist = epq_pf_plots,
  labels = "auto", ncol = 2, label_size = 20,
  align = "hv", label_fontface = "plain"
)

ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_personfit.png",
       epq_personfit_plot, width = 12, height = 10, dpi = 300)



# ==========================================================
# 8. PERSON-ITEM (WRIGHT) MAPS: EPQ-RS-B subscales (2PL)
# ==========================================================

epq_wright_plots <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_models)) {
  mod <- epq_models[[sc]]
  
  # Extract person theta estimates
  theta_scores <- fscores(mod, full.scores.SE = TRUE)[, 1]
  person_df <- data.frame(Theta = theta_scores)
  
  # Extract item difficulty (b) parameters
  pars <- coef(mod, simplify = TRUE)$items
  item_names <- rownames(pars)
  b_vals <- pars[, "d"]        # intercept in mirt parameterisation
  a_vals <- pars[, "a1"]
  # Convert mirt intercept (d) to traditional difficulty: b = -d/a
  b_traditional <- -b_vals / a_vals
  
  item_df <- data.frame(
    Item = item_names,
    Difficulty = b_traditional
  )
  item_df$Item <- factor(item_df$Item, levels = item_df$Item[order(item_df$Difficulty)])
  
  # Person distribution (left panel)
  p_person <- ggplot(person_df, aes(x = Theta)) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.3, fill = scale_fills[[sc]], alpha = 0.6,
                   col = I("black")) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-4, 4), breaks = seq(-4, 4, 1)) +
    labs(x = expression(theta), y = "Frequency",
         title = "Person Distribution") +
    apatheme

  # Item map (right panel)
  p_items <- ggplot(item_df, aes(x = Difficulty, y = Item)) +
    geom_point(size = 3, colour = scale_colors[[sc]]) +
    geom_segment(aes(x = min(person_df$Theta, item_df$Difficulty) - 0.5,
                     xend = Difficulty, y = Item, yend = Item),
                 linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-4, 4), breaks = seq(-4, 4, 1)) +
    labs(x = expression(theta), y = NULL,
         title = "Item Difficulty (b)") +
    apatheme

  # Combine side by side
  epq_wright_plots[[sc]] <- cowplot::plot_grid(
    p_person, p_items,
    ncol = 2, rel_widths = c(0.4, 0.6),
    labels = paste0(sc), label_size = 12, label_fontface = "bold"
  )

  ggsave(paste0("outputs/plots/EPQ_WrightMap_", sc, ".png"),
         epq_wright_plots[[sc]], width = 12, height = 6, dpi = 300)
}

# Combined composite of all four Wright maps
epq_wright_composite <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  plotlist = epq_wright_plots,
  ncol = 1, labels = "auto", label_size = 20,
  label_fontface = "plain"
)

ggsave("outputs/plots/EPQ_WrightMap_composite.png",
       epq_wright_composite, width = 12, height = 24, dpi = 300)
```


Across the 48 items, item fit was generally acceptable. All infit and out fit mean-square values fell within the range except for  one Lie scale item (EYP12).  RMSEA.S-X² values remained below .10 in all cases.
Item discrimination and difficulty parameters are reported in Table [X]. The four subscales differed markedly in the quality of their item parameters. Neuroticism exhibited the strongest and most homogeneous discrimination values. All 12 items achieved moderate to very high discrimination (a range = 1.15 to 3.08), with eight items classified as very high (a ≥ 1.70). Difficulty parameters were well distributed across the θ continuum, ranging from -1.78  to 1.33.

Extraversion items showed adequate discrimination overall (a range = 0.71 to 2.23), with three items classified as very high and three as high. Difficulty values ranged from -2.49  to 1.94, although difficulty values were somewhat concentrated below zero, suggesting that most items are relatively easy to endorse.

The Lie scale displayed a heterogeneous discrimination profile. Several items reached very high discrimination (EYP8, a = 2.02; EYP12, a = 2.47; EYP20, a = 1.71; EYP29, a = 1.80), but three items exhibited very low to low discrimination (EYP4, a = 0.29; EYP16, a = 0.61; EYP45, a = 0.59). Notably, EYP4 had an extreme difficulty value of -9.71, placing it far outside the observable θ range and rendering it essentially non-informative.

Psychoticism was the most problematic subscale. Discrimination values were highly heterogeneous, ranging from -0.07 (EYP2) to 1.76 (EYP18). EYP2 returned a negative discrimination estimate, indicating that endorsement probability decreased with increasing levels of the latent trait, a result that raises concerns about item keying or construct relevance in this sample. Two additional items (EYP31, a = 0.29; EYP6, a = 0.25) were classified as very low, contributing negligible measurement information. Difficulty parameters were predominantly high and positive (b range = 0.28 to 8.49 for positively discriminating items), indicating that most Psychoticism items are endorsed only at very elevated trait levels. EYP6 had an extreme difficulty value of 8.49, placing it well beyond the meaningful measurement range.


Marginal reliability coefficients were .83 for Neuroticism, .76 for Extraversion, .76 for the Lie scale, and .44 for Psychoticism. Empirical reliability estimates were .80, .70, .69, and -.22, respectively. The negative empirical reliability for Psychoticism indicates that the observed score variance attributable to measurement error exceeded the variance attributable to true score differences, rendering individual-level Psychoticism scores uninterpretable.

The TIC for Neuroticism peaked at 9.52 units of information (minimum SE = 0.32), and the scale maintained reliable measurement (marginal reliability ≥ .70) across a broad θ range from -1.55 to 1.66, spanning approximately 3.2 standard deviation units. Extraversion peaked at 5.26 (minimum SE = 0.44), with reliable measurement from θ = -1.99 to 0.99. The Lie scale peaked at 5.25 (minimum SE = 0.44), with reliable measurement from θ = -1.71 to 1.00. Psychoticism peaked at only 2.78 (minimum SE = 0.60) and achieved marginal reliability ≥ .70 only within the narrow range of θ = 1.65 to 2.87, a 1.2 standard deviation window located well above the population mean. This means that Psychoticism scores are reliable only for individuals approximately 1.5 to 3 standard deviations above the trait mean, a region containing a small fraction of any normative sample

### Person-Item Maps (Wright Maps)
Wright maps for the four EPQ-RS-B subscales are presented in Figure [X]. These maps juxtapose the distribution of person theta estimates (left panel) with item difficulty parameters (right panel) on a common metric, allowing visual evaluation of person-item targeting.
For Extraversion, the person distribution was approximately normal and centered near zero, with the bulk of respondents falling between θ = -2 and θ = 2. Item difficulties spanned a broad range from approximately -3 (EYP7) to 2 (EYP36), with reasonable coverage across the person distribution. Most items clustered between -1 and 1, overlapping well with the densest region of the person distribution. Two items (EYP3 and EYP36) had difficulty values above 1.5, providing some differentiation at the upper end of the trait continuum. Conversely, EYP7 and EYP32 anchored the lower end near -3 and -2 respectively. Overall, the Extraversion items demonstrated adequate targeting, though coverage was somewhat denser in the low-to-moderate range than at the positive extreme.
For Neuroticism, the person distribution was similarly centered near zero with a slight negative skew. Item difficulties ranged from approximately -2 (EYP34) to nearly 4 (EYP30). The majority of items fell between -1 and 1, covering the central region of the person distribution effectively. However, two items (EYP9 and EYP30) had difficulty values exceeding 3, placing them well beyond the upper boundary where most respondents were located. These items would only be endorsed by individuals with very high levels of neuroticism, and they contribute information almost exclusively at the positive extreme. Despite these outliers, the remaining ten items provided graduated coverage across the low-to-moderate trait range. The presence of EYP34 at roughly -2 ensured some measurement capacity at the lower end.
For the Lie scale, the person distribution was centered slightly below zero with most respondents falling between -2 and 1. Item difficulties spread from approximately -3 (EYP45) to nearly 4 (EYP47). Two items (EYP16 and EYP47) had difficulty values above 3, far exceeding the upper bound of the person distribution and therefore contributing negligible information for all but the most extreme scorers. At the opposite end, EYP45 and EYP40 had difficulties near -3 and -2, capturing variation among low scorers. The middle range was covered by a cluster of items between -1 and 0 (EYP8, EYP12, EYP37, EYP33). This pattern reveals a gap in item coverage between approximately 0 and 2.5, meaning the scale differentiates poorly among moderately high scorers on social desirability.
For Psychoticism, the person distribution was strongly negatively skewed, with the modal category near θ = -0.5 and a long positive tail. The overwhelming majority of respondents were concentrated between -1 and 0.5. In stark contrast, most item difficulties fell between 0 and 3, with EYP35 near 2.5 being the most difficult. Only two items (EYP14 at approximately -2 and EYP31 near -1) overlapped with the densest region of the person distribution. Three items (EYP2, EYP6, and EYP28) had no visible difficulty points on the plot, suggesting extremely high or computationally unstable difficulty estimates. This severe mismatch between person locations and item difficulties means the Psychoticism subscale provides almost no measurement precision for the majority of respondents, who score in the low-to-average range. The scale functions, in effect, as a set of very "hard" items that only differentiate among the small minority of individuals at the upper extreme of the trait.


```{r theta, include=FALSE}
# ==========================================================
# Find theta range where I(theta) >= SE(theta)
# ==========================================================
theta_range <- seq(-4, 4, by = 0.01)

epq_precision_range <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(epq_models), function(sc) {
  info <- as.numeric(testinfo(epq_models[[sc]], Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1)))
  se   <- 1 / sqrt(info)
  
  # Theta values where information exceeds SE
  precise <- theta_range[info >= se]
  
  data.frame(
    Scale     = sc,
    Theta_min = round(min(precise), 2),
    Theta_max = round(max(precise), 2),
    Peak_info = round(max(info), 3),
    Min_SE    = round(min(se), 3)
  )
}))

epq_precision_range
readr::write_csv(epq_precision_range, "outputs/tables/epq_precision_range.csv")

```

```{r marginaltehta, include=FALSE}
epq_reliable_range <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(epq_models), function(sc) {
  info <- as.numeric(testinfo(epq_models[[sc]], Theta = matrix(theta_range, ncol = 1)))
  rel  <- info / (1 + info)
  
  reliable <- theta_range[rel >= 0.70]
  
  data.frame(
    Scale     = sc,
    Theta_min = round(min(reliable), 2),
    Theta_max = round(max(reliable), 2),
    Peak_rel  = round(max(rel), 3),
    Info_at_70 = round(0.70 / (1 - 0.70), 3)  # = 2.333, for reference
  )
}))

epq_reliable_range
readr::write_csv(epq_reliable_range, "outputs/tables/epq_marginal_reliable_range.csv")

```



# Differential Item Functioning

Gender DIF was examined for each EPQ-RS subscale using the Rasch model estimated via conditional maximum likelihood. Persons with extreme total scores (all items endorsed or none endorsed) were excluded from the CML estimation, as these response patterns are uninformative under the conditional framework. The reference group was female and the focal group was male. The Andersen likelihood ratio (LR) test assessed omnibus measurement invariance, and item-level Wald tests, with BH-adjusted p values, identified specific items exhibiting DIF. Effect sizes were expressed as the difference in group-specific Rasch difficulty parameters (delta-beta), with positive values indicating that an item was more difficult (less likely to be endorsed) for males relative to females. Results are presented in Table X.
The omnibus Andersen LR test was statistically significant for all four scales: Psychoticism (LR = 34.56, df = 11, p < .001), Extraversion (LR = 36.64, df = 11, p < .001), Neuroticism (LR = 48.77, df = 11, p < .001), and Lie (LR = 30.98, df = 11, p = .001). These results indicate that perfect measurement invariance across gender does not hold for any scale at the omnibus level. However, omnibus significance does not specify which items are responsible, nor does it establish practical importance; item-level tests are needed to localise and quantify the effects.
Psychoticism. After BH adjustment, one item was flagged: EYP43 (z = 4.02, adjusted p < .001, delta-beta = 0.78), indicating that this item was substantially harder to endorse for males than for females at the same trait level. Several other items showed unadjusted p values below .05 (EYP14, p = .014; EYP18, p = .034; EYP26, p = .025), but none survived correction (adjusted p values: .082, .101, .100, respectively). The remaining items showed negligible difficulty differences across groups (|delta-beta| range: 0.02 to 0.56).

Extraversion. One item was flagged: EYP48 (z = -5.35, adjusted p < .001, delta-beta = -1.07), indicating that this item was considerably easier to endorse for males at equivalent trait levels. This was the largest item-level effect observed across all four scales. No other Extraversion item approached significance after adjustment, and 9 of the remaining 11 items showed difficulty differences of 0.27 or smaller.
Neuroticism. This scale showed the most extensive DIF, with five items flagged after BH adjustment. EYP9 (z = -3.89, adjusted p < .001, delta-beta = -0.99) and EYP13 (z = 3.93, adjusted p < .001, delta-beta = 0.94) displayed the largest effects, with EYP9 being easier and EYP13 being harder to endorse for males. Three additional items were flagged: EYP17 (z = 2.77, adjusted p = .022, delta-beta = 0.60), EYP38 (z = 2.32, adjusted p = .048, delta-beta = 0.52), and EYP42 (z = -2.37, adjusted p = .048, delta-beta = -0.50). The direction of these effects was mixed: EYP17 and EYP38 were harder for males, whereas EYP42 was easier. The remaining seven items showed no evidence of DIF (adjusted p values ranging from .109 to .981).
Lie. Despite the significant omnibus LR test (LR = 30.98, p = .001), no individual item reached significance after BH adjustment. Several items approached conventional thresholds at the unadjusted level (EYP12, p = .009; EYP20, p = .013; EYP40, p = .022; EYP16, p = .040), but all adjusted p values exceeded .05 (range: .079 to .973). The difficulty differences for these items ranged from |0.42| to |0.56|, suggesting modest but non-significant trends.
In summary, 7 of 48 items displayed statistically significant gender DIF after correction: 1 on Psychoticism (EYP43), 1 on Extraversion (EYP48), 5 on Neuroticism (EYP9, EYP13, EYP17, EYP38, EYP42), and none on the Lie scale. The Neuroticism scale exhibited both the strongest omnibus departure from invariance and the greatest number of individually flagged items.

```{r DIF, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================ Not in Use
# EPQ DIF Analysis (Gender) -- full script
# Uses printed Raju output extraction
# Stores raw Lord and Raju results for each subscale
# ============================================================

library(difR)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)
library(readr)

# ---------------------------
# Settings
# ---------------------------
use_purify <- TRUE
max_purify_iter <- 10

# ============================================================
# Helper function:
# Extract Raju table from printed output
# ============================================================
extract_raju_from_print <- function(x) {
  txt <- capture.output(print(x))
  
  start_line <- grep("^Raju's statistic:", txt)
  end_line   <- grep("^Signif\\. codes:", txt)
  
  if (length(start_line) == 0) {
    stop("Could not find 'Raju's statistic:' in printed output.")
  }
  if (length(end_line) == 0) {
    stop("Could not find 'Signif. codes:' in printed output.")
  }
  
  # Keep only the printed table block
  block <- txt[(start_line + 1):(end_line - 1)]
  block <- block[nzchar(trimws(block))]
  
  # Remove table header
  block <- block[!grepl("^\\s*Stat\\.\\s+P-value\\s+Adj\\. P", block)]
  
  # Parse each line of the printed table
  out_list <- lapply(block, function(line) {
    line <- trimws(line)
    
    # Remove trailing significance symbols (*, **, ***, .)
    line_clean <- sub("\\s+[*\\.]+\\s*$", "", line)
    
    parts <- strsplit(line_clean, "\\s+")[[1]]
    
    if (length(parts) < 4) {
      return(NULL)
    }
    
    data.frame(
      Item       = parts[1],
      Raju_Z     = as.numeric(parts[2]),
      Raju_p     = as.numeric(parts[3]),
      Raju_p_adj = as.numeric(parts[4]),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  })
  
  out <- do.call(rbind, out_list)
  
  if (is.null(out) || nrow(out) == 0) {
    stop("Could not parse any rows from printed Raju output.")
  }
  
  # Extract flagged DIF items from the printed output
  dif_line <- grep("^Items detected as DIF items:", txt)
  flagged_items <- character(0)
  
  if (length(dif_line) > 0) {
    tail_block <- txt[(dif_line + 1):length(txt)]
    tail_block <- tail_block[nzchar(trimws(tail_block))]
    
    if (length(tail_block) > 0) {
      flagged_items <- unlist(strsplit(paste(tail_block, collapse = " "), "\\s+"))
      flagged_items <- flagged_items[nzchar(flagged_items)]
    }
  }
  
  out$Raju_flag <- ifelse(out$Item %in% flagged_items, "DIF", "")
  
  out
}

# ============================================================
# Objects to store everything
# ============================================================
dif_inputs   <- list()
lord_objects <- list()
raju_objects <- list()

lord_tables  <- list()
raju_tables  <- list()
scale_tables <- list()

# ============================================================
# Run DIF analysis for each EPQ subscale
# ============================================================
for (sc in names(epq_item_sets)) {
  
  cat("\n=====================================\n")
  cat("Running DIF for:", sc, "\n")
  cat("=====================================\n")
  
  # ---------------------------
  # Step 1: subset scale data
  # ---------------------------
  dat   <- as.data.frame(epq_items[, epq_item_sets[[sc]], drop = FALSE])
  group <- demographics$gender
  
  # ---------------------------
  # Step 2: complete cases only
  # ---------------------------
  keep  <- complete.cases(dat, group)
  dat   <- dat[keep, , drop = FALSE]
  group <- factor(group[keep])
  
  if (nlevels(group) < 2) {
    message("Skipping ", sc, ": gender has fewer than 2 groups.")
    next
  }
  
  items   <- names(dat)
  n_items <- length(items)
  focal   <- levels(group)[2]
  
  # Save clean inputs so you can inspect later
  dif_inputs[[sc]] <- list(
    dat = dat,
    group = group,
    focal = focal,
    items = items
  )
  
  # ============================================================
  # LORD
  # ============================================================
  dif_lord <- tryCatch(
    difLord(
      Data            = dat,
      group           = group,
      focal.name      = focal,
      model           = "2PL",
      purify          = use_purify,
      nrIter          = max_purify_iter,
      p.adjust.method = "BH"
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      message("Lord failed for ", sc, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  
  lord_objects[[sc]] <- dif_lord
  
  if (!is.null(dif_lord)) {
    
    lord_chi <- as.numeric(dif_lord$LordChi)
    lord_p   <- as.numeric(dif_lord$p.value)
    
    if (!is.null(dif_lord$adjusted.p)) {
      lord_p_adj <- as.numeric(dif_lord$adjusted.p)
    } else {
      lord_p_adj <- p.adjust(lord_p, method = "BH")
    }
    
    lord_flagged <- dif_lord$DIFitems
    if (is.null(lord_flagged) || identical(lord_flagged, "No DIF item detected")) {
      lord_flagged <- integer(0)
    }
    
    if (!is.null(dif_lord$convergence)) {
      if (isTRUE(dif_lord$convergence)) {
        lord_convergence <- "converged"
      } else {
        lord_convergence <- "not converged"
      }
    } else {
      lord_convergence <- NA_character_
    }
    
    if (!is.null(dif_lord$nrPur)) {
      lord_nrPur <- as.numeric(dif_lord$nrPur)
    } else {
      lord_nrPur <- NA_real_
    }
    
    if (!is.null(dif_lord$itemParFinal)) {
      all_pars <- dif_lord$itemParFinal
    } else {
      all_pars <- dif_lord$itemParInit
    }
    
    pars_ref <- all_pars[1:n_items, , drop = FALSE]
    pars_foc <- all_pars[(n_items + 1):(2 * n_items), , drop = FALSE]
    
    a_ref <- as.numeric(pars_ref[, 1])
    b_ref <- as.numeric(pars_ref[, 2])
    a_foc <- as.numeric(pars_foc[, 1])
    b_foc <- as.numeric(pars_foc[, 2])
    
    delta_b <- b_foc - b_ref
    
    ETS_class <- ifelse(
      is.na(delta_b), "",
      ifelse(abs(delta_b) < 0.43, "A (Negligible)",
      ifelse(abs(delta_b) < 0.64, "B (Moderate)",
                                 "C (Large)"))
    )
    
    lord_tbl <- tibble(
      Scale            = sc,
      Item             = items,
      Lord_status      = "completed",
      Lord_convergence = lord_convergence,
      Lord_nrPur       = lord_nrPur,
      Lord_X2          = lord_chi,
      Lord_p           = lord_p,
      Lord_p_adj       = lord_p_adj,
      Lord_flag        = ifelse(seq_len(n_items) %in% lord_flagged, "DIF", ""),
      a_ref            = a_ref,
      b_ref            = b_ref,
      a_foc            = a_foc,
      b_foc            = b_foc,
      delta_b          = delta_b,
      ETS_class        = ETS_class
    )
    
  } else {
    
    lord_tbl <- tibble(
      Scale            = sc,
      Item             = items,
      Lord_status      = "failed",
      Lord_convergence = NA_character_,
      Lord_nrPur       = NA_real_,
      Lord_X2          = NA_real_,
      Lord_p           = NA_real_,
      Lord_p_adj       = NA_real_,
      Lord_flag        = "",
      a_ref            = NA_real_,
      b_ref            = NA_real_,
      a_foc            = NA_real_,
      b_foc            = NA_real_,
      delta_b          = NA_real_,
      ETS_class        = ""
    )
  }
  
  lord_tables[[sc]] <- lord_tbl
  
  # ============================================================
  # RAJU
  # ============================================================
  dif_raju <- tryCatch(
    difRaju(
      Data            = dat,
      group           = group,
      focal.name      = focal,
      model           = "2PL",
      purify          = use_purify,
      nrIter          = max_purify_iter,
      p.adjust.method = "BH"
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      message("Raju failed for ", sc, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  
  raju_objects[[sc]] <- dif_raju
  
  if (!is.null(dif_raju)) {
    
    # This uses the printed output, not dif_raju$p.value
    raju_tbl <- extract_raju_from_print(dif_raju)
    
    # Add scale name
    raju_tbl$Scale <- sc
    raju_tbl <- raju_tbl %>%
      select(Scale, everything())
    
    # Add convergence info
    if (!is.null(dif_raju$convergence)) {
      if (isTRUE(dif_raju$convergence)) {
        raju_convergence <- "converged"
      } else {
        raju_convergence <- "not converged"
      }
    } else {
      raju_convergence <- NA_character_
    }
    
    if (!is.null(dif_raju$nrPur)) {
      raju_nrPur <- as.numeric(dif_raju$nrPur)
    } else {
      raju_nrPur <- NA_real_
    }
    
    raju_tbl <- raju_tbl %>%
      mutate(
        Raju_status      = "completed",
        Raju_convergence = raju_convergence,
        Raju_nrPur       = raju_nrPur,
        .before = Raju_Z
      )
    
  } else {
    
    raju_tbl <- tibble(
      Scale            = sc,
      Item             = items,
      Raju_status      = "failed",
      Raju_convergence = NA_character_,
      Raju_nrPur       = NA_real_,
      Raju_Z           = NA_real_,
      Raju_p           = NA_real_,
      Raju_p_adj       = NA_real_,
      Raju_flag        = ""
    )
  }
  
  raju_tables[[sc]] <- raju_tbl
  
  # ============================================================
  # Merge Lord + Raju for this subscale
  # ============================================================
  scale_tbl <- left_join(
    lord_tbl,
    raju_tbl,
    by = c("Scale", "Item")
  )
  
  scale_tables[[sc]] <- scale_tbl
}

# ============================================================
# Final combined table
# ============================================================
epq_dif_all <- bind_rows(scale_tables)

# View final result
epq_dif_all

# ============================================================
# Save results
# ============================================================
# write_csv(epq_dif_all, "EPQ_DIF_gender_all_scales.csv")

# # Save each scale separately
# for (sc in names(scale_tables)) {
#   write_csv(
#     scale_tables[[sc]],
#     paste0("EPQ_DIF_gender_", sc, ".csv")
#   )
# }

#Inspect

# lord_objects$Psychoticism
# raju_objects$Psychoticism
# 
# lord_tables$Psychoticism
# raju_tables$Psychoticism
# scale_tables$Psychoticism
# 
# lord_objects$Extraversion
# raju_objects$Extraversion
# 
# lord_tables$Extraversion
# raju_tables$Extraversion
# scale_tables$Extraversion
# 
# 
# lord_objects$Neuroticism
# raju_objects$Neuroticism
# 
# lord_tables$Neuroticism
# raju_tables$Neuroticism
# scale_tables$Neuroticism
# 
# lord_objects$Lie
# raju_objects$Lie
# 
# lord_tables$Lie
# raju_tables$Lie
# scale_tables$Lie

```

```{r DIFRash, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# EPQ-RS DIF Analysis (Gender) — Rasch Model, Joint Calibration
# Method: Conditional ML Rasch (eRm) + Wald test & Andersen LR test
# ============================================================

library(eRm)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)
library(readr)

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 0. Helper: format p values APA style
# ---------------------------------------------------------
fmt_p <- function(p) {
  ifelse(
    is.na(p), NA_character_,
    ifelse(p < .001, "< .001", sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", p)))
  )
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Prepare data
#    Adjust the object names below to match your workspace.
#    epq_items        : data.frame of all 48 binary item responses
#    demographics     : data.frame with a column "gender"
#    epq_item_sets    : named list mapping scale names to item columns
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# Example structure (uncomment / adapt as needed):
# epq_item_sets <- list(
#   Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP14","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39",
#                     "EYP2","EYP6","EYP18","EYP26","EYP28",
#                     "EYP35","EYP43"),
#   Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19",
#                     "EYP23","EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48",
#                     "EYP27","EYP41"),
#   Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17",
#                     "EYP21","EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38",
#                     "EYP42","EYP46")
# )

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Analysis loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------
rasch_dif_results <- list()

for (sc in names(epq_item_sets)) {
  
  cat("\n========================================\n")
  cat("Scale:", sc, "\n")
  cat("========================================\n")
  
  items_vec <- epq_item_sets[[sc]]
  dat       <- as.data.frame(epq_items[, items_vec, drop = FALSE])
  group     <- demographics$gender
  
  # Complete cases only
  keep  <- complete.cases(dat, group)
  dat   <- dat[keep, , drop = FALSE]
  group <- factor(group[keep])
  
  if (nlevels(group) < 2) {
    message("Skipping ", sc, ": fewer than 2 gender groups.")
    next
  }
  
  n_items <- ncol(dat)
  items   <- names(dat)
  
  # --- Remove persons with perfect (all-0 or all-12) scores ---
  # CML estimation conditions on total scores; extreme scores
  # are not informative and must be excluded.
  row_sums <- rowSums(dat)
  non_extreme <- row_sums > 0 & row_sums < n_items
  
  dat_cml   <- dat[non_extreme, , drop = FALSE]
  group_cml <- group[non_extreme]
  
  cat("  N after removing extreme scores:", nrow(dat_cml), "\n")
  cat("  N removed (extreme):", sum(!non_extreme), "\n")
  cat("  Group sizes:", table(group_cml), "\n")
  
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  # 2a. Fit the joint Rasch model (CML)
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  rasch_fit <- tryCatch(
    RM(dat_cml),
    error = function(e) {
      message("  Rasch model failed for ", sc, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  
  if (is.null(rasch_fit)) next
  
  # Item difficulty (beta) parameters — note eRm uses the
  # "easiness" parameterisation by default; we reverse sign
  # for conventional difficulty interpretation.
  beta <- -coef(rasch_fit)  # positive = harder
  
  cat("  Item difficulties (beta, higher = harder):\n")
  print(round(beta, 3))
  
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  # 2b. Wald test for DIF — split by gender
  #     Tests H0: item parameters are equal across groups
  #     One test per item; overall omnibus test also provided.
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  wald_res <- tryCatch(
    Waldtest(rasch_fit, splitcr = group_cml),
    error = function(e) {
      message("  Waldtest failed for ", sc, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  # 2c. Andersen LR test — split by gender
  #     Omnibus test of measurement invariance across groups.
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  lr_res <- tryCatch(
    LRtest(rasch_fit, splitcr = group_cml),
    error = function(e) {
      message("  LRtest failed for ", sc, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  
  if (!is.null(lr_res)) {
    cat("\n  Andersen LR test (omnibus):\n")
    cat("    LR =", round(lr_res$LR, 2),
        "  df =", lr_res$df,
        "  p =", fmt_p(lr_res$pvalue), "\n")
  }
  
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  # 2d. Compute group-specific item difficulties
  #     Fit separate Rasch models to extract beta per group.
  #     This is for DESCRIPTIVE purposes only; the DIF test
  #     itself uses the joint-calibration Wald/LR approach.
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  lev <- levels(group_cml)
  
  rasch_g1 <- tryCatch(RM(dat_cml[group_cml == lev[1], ]), error = function(e) NULL)
  rasch_g2 <- tryCatch(RM(dat_cml[group_cml == lev[2], ]), error = function(e) NULL)
  
  beta_g1 <- if (!is.null(rasch_g1)) -coef(rasch_g1) else rep(NA, n_items)
  beta_g2 <- if (!is.null(rasch_g2)) -coef(rasch_g2) else rep(NA, n_items)
  
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  # 2e. Assemble results table
  # ---------------------------------------------------------
  
  # Joint betas: eRm may return K-1 or K params depending on version.
  # Standardise to length K (n_items) with sum-zero constraint.
  raw_beta <- -coef(rasch_fit)
  if (length(raw_beta) == n_items - 1) {
    beta_joint_full <- c(raw_beta, -sum(raw_beta))
    names(beta_joint_full) <- items
  } else {
    beta_joint_full <- raw_beta[1:n_items]
    names(beta_joint_full) <- items
  }
  
  # Wald test: extract per-item z and p.
  # eRm returns all K items in model order; use positional indexing.
  if (!is.null(wald_res)) {
    wald_tab    <- wald_res$coef.table
    wald_z_full <- wald_tab[, "z-statistic"]
    wald_p_full <- wald_tab[, "p-value"]
  } else {
    wald_z_full <- rep(NA_real_, n_items)
    wald_p_full <- rep(NA_real_, n_items)
  }
  
  # BH adjustment
  wald_p_adj_full <- p.adjust(wald_p_full, method = "BH")
  
  # Group-specific betas: standardise to length K
  if (length(beta_g1) == n_items - 1) {
    beta_g1_full <- c(beta_g1, -sum(beta_g1))
  } else {
    beta_g1_full <- beta_g1[1:n_items]
  }
  if (length(beta_g2) == n_items - 1) {
    beta_g2_full <- c(beta_g2, -sum(beta_g2))
  } else {
    beta_g2_full <- beta_g2[1:n_items]
  }
  names(beta_g1_full) <- items
  names(beta_g2_full) <- items
  
  delta_beta <- beta_g2_full - beta_g1_full
  
  tbl <- tibble(
    Scale            = sc,
    Item             = items,
    beta_joint       = as.numeric(beta_joint_full),
    beta_ref         = as.numeric(beta_g1_full),
    beta_focal       = as.numeric(beta_g2_full),
    delta_beta       = as.numeric(delta_beta),
    Wald_z           = wald_z_full,
    Wald_p           = wald_p_full,
    Wald_p_adj       = wald_p_adj_full,
    Wald_DIF         = ifelse(wald_p_adj_full < .05, "DIF", ""),
    LR_chi2          = if (!is.null(lr_res)) lr_res$LR else NA_real_,
    LR_df            = if (!is.null(lr_res)) lr_res$df  else NA_integer_,
    LR_p             = if (!is.null(lr_res)) lr_res$pvalue else NA_real_
  )
  
  rasch_dif_results[[sc]] <- tbl
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Combine and format for APA reporting
# ---------------------------------------------------------
rasch_dif_all <- bind_rows(rasch_dif_results)

rasch_dif_apa <- rasch_dif_all %>%
  transmute(
    Scale,
    Item,
    `beta (joint)`    = sprintf("%.2f", beta_joint),
    `beta (Ref)`      = sprintf("%.2f", beta_ref),
    `beta (Focal)`    = sprintf("%.2f", beta_focal),
    `Delta beta`      = sprintf("%.2f", delta_beta),
    `Wald z`          = sprintf("%.2f", Wald_z),
    `Wald p`          = fmt_p(Wald_p),
    `Wald p (adj)`    = fmt_p(Wald_p_adj),
    `DIF flag`        = Wald_DIF,
    `LR chi2 (omnibus)` = sprintf("%.2f", LR_chi2),
    `LR df`           = as.character(LR_df),
    `LR p (omnibus)`  = fmt_p(LR_p)
  )

readr::write_csv(rasch_dif_apa, "outputs/tables/EPQ_DIF_gender_Rasch_APA.csv")

cat("\n\nDone. Results saved to outputs/tables/EPQ_DIF_gender_Rasch_APA.csv\n")

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Print summary to console
# ---------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n========= SUMMARY =========\n")
for (sc in names(rasch_dif_results)) {
  tbl <- rasch_dif_results[[sc]]
  flagged <- tbl %>% filter(Wald_DIF == "DIF") %>% pull(Item)
  cat("\n", sc, ":\n")
  cat("  Andersen LR (omnibus): chi2 =", round(tbl$LR_chi2[1], 2),
      ", df =", tbl$LR_df[1],
      ", p =", fmt_p(tbl$LR_p[1]), "\n")
  if (length(flagged) > 0) {
    cat("  Items flagged (Wald, BH-adj p < .05):", paste(flagged, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  } else {
    cat("  No items flagged after BH adjustment.\n")
  }
}
```

```{r eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# ---------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Print summary to console
# ---------------------------------------------------------
cat("\n========= SUMMARY =========\n")
for (sc in names(rasch_dif_results)) {
  tbl <- rasch_dif_results[[sc]]
  flagged <- tbl %>% filter(Wald_DIF == "DIF") %>% pull(Item)
  cat("\n", sc, ":\n")
  cat("  Andersen LR (omnibus): chi2 =", round(tbl$LR_chi2[1], 2),
      ", df =", tbl$LR_df[1],
      ", p =", fmt_p(tbl$LR_p[1]), "\n")
  if (length(flagged) > 0) {
    cat("  Items flagged (Wald, BH-adj p < .05):", paste(flagged, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  } else {
    cat("  No items flagged after BH adjustment.\n")
  }
}
```

```{r 2PLDIF}
library(mirt)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)

fmt_p <- function(p) {
  dplyr::case_when(
    is.na(p) ~ NA_character_,
    p < .001 ~ "< .001",
    TRUE     ~ sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", p))
  )
}

# ============================================================
# Example: Neuroticism
# ============================================================

sc <- "Neuroticism"
ref_group <- "Female"

items_vec <- epq_item_sets[[sc]]

dat_raw <- as.data.frame(epq_items[, items_vec, drop = FALSE])
grp_raw <- demographics$gender

keep <- complete.cases(dat_raw, grp_raw)
dat  <- dat_raw[keep, , drop = FALSE]
grp  <- droplevels(factor(grp_raw[keep]))

if (ref_group %in% levels(grp)) {
  grp <- relevel(grp, ref = ref_group)
}

dat[] <- lapply(dat, function(x) as.numeric(as.character(x)))

# Keep binary items varying within both groups
item_ok <- vapply(names(dat), function(nm) {
  x <- dat[[nm]]
  binary_ok <- all(na.omit(x) %in% c(0, 1))
  var_by_group <- tapply(x, grp, function(z) length(unique(na.omit(z))) > 1)
  binary_ok && all(var_by_group)
}, logical(1))

dat <- dat[, item_ok, drop = FALSE]

cat("N =", nrow(dat), "\n")
print(table(grp))
cat("Items retained:", paste(names(dat), collapse = ", "), "\n")

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 1. Initial anchored baseline model
# All items constrained equal across groups
# Means/variances free in focal group
# ------------------------------------------------------------
mg_base <- multipleGroup(
  data       = dat,
  model      = 1,
  group      = grp,
  itemtype   = "2PL",
  invariance = c("free_means", "free_var", colnames(dat)),
  SE         = FALSE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 2. DIF tests
# ------------------------------------------------------------
dif_uniform <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = mg_base,
  which.par  = "d",
  items2test = colnames(dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

dif_nonuniform <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = mg_base,
  which.par  = "a1",
  items2test = colnames(dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

dif_both <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = mg_base,
  which.par  = c("a1", "d"),
  items2test = colnames(dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

# Convert combined DIF to table
dif_both_df <- as.data.frame(dif_both)
dif_both_df$Item <- rownames(dif_both_df)

dif_both_tbl <- tibble(
  Scale     = sc,
  Item      = dif_both_df$Item,
  X2        = dif_both_df$X2,
  df        = dif_both_df$df,
  p         = dif_both_df$p,
  adj_p     = dif_both_df$adj_p,
  p_fmt     = fmt_p(dif_both_df$p),
  adj_p_fmt = fmt_p(dif_both_df$adj_p),
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(dif_both_df$adj_p) & dif_both_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

print(dif_both_tbl)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 3. Define anchor items from non-flagged combined DIF
# ------------------------------------------------------------
anchor_items <- dif_both_tbl %>%
  filter(DIF_flag == "No") %>%
  pull(Item)

flagged_items <- dif_both_tbl %>%
  filter(DIF_flag == "Flagged") %>%
  pull(Item)

cat("Anchor items:", paste(anchor_items, collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("Flagged items:", paste(flagged_items, collapse = ", "), "\n")

# Need at least 1-2 anchors; ideally more
if (length(anchor_items) < 2) {
  stop("Too few anchor items for an identified partially anchored model.")
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 4. Partially anchored model
# Anchor items constrained equal
# Flagged items free across groups
# ------------------------------------------------------------
mg_anchor <- multipleGroup(
  data       = dat,
  model      = 1,
  group      = grp,
  itemtype   = "2PL",
  invariance = c("free_means", "free_var", anchor_items),
  SE         = FALSE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 5. Extract group-specific parameters
# NOTE:
# - anchor items will be equal across groups by design
# - flagged items can differ across groups
# ------------------------------------------------------------
pars_anchor <- coef(mg_anchor, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)

group_item_pars <- bind_rows(
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(pars_anchor[[levels(grp)[1]]]$items)
    x$Item  <- rownames(x)
    x$Group <- levels(grp)[1]
    x$Scale <- sc
    x
  },
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(pars_anchor[[levels(grp)[2]]]$items)
    x$Item  <- rownames(x)
    x$Group <- levels(grp)[2]
    x$Scale <- sc
    x
  }
)

print(group_item_pars)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 6. Optional omnibus comparison:
# Compare fully anchored baseline vs partially anchored model
# This is an identified omnibus test of whether freeing flagged
# items improves fit.
# ------------------------------------------------------------
omni <- as.data.frame(anova(mg_base, mg_anchor))
print(omni)
```


## Concurrent Validity With BFI-2

```{r concurrent-validity, include=FALSE}
cross_framework_cor <- psych::corr.test(
  bfi_domains,
  epq_scores,
  method = "pearson",
  adjust = "holm"
)

cross_r <- as.data.frame(round(cross_framework_cor$r, 3)) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("BFI-2 Domain")

cross_p <- as.data.frame(cross_framework_cor$p) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("BFI-2 Domain")
```

Table \@ref(tab:concurrent) presents the correlation matrix between EPQ-RS-B subscale scores and BFI-2-B domain scores. The concurrent validity pattern conformed to theoretical expectations.

```{r concurrent, results='asis'}
apa_table(
  cross_r,
  caption = "Pearson correlations between BFI-2-B domains and EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "All coefficients Holm-adjusted. Bold values indicate convergent validity pairs."
)
```

# Discussion

The present study provides the first psychometric evaluation of a Bangla adaptation of the EPQ-RS. The results largely replicate the cross-cultural pattern that has emerged across more than a dozen language adaptations over four decades: Extraversion and Neuroticism subscales demonstrated acceptable reliability, coherent factor structure, and adequate measurement precision, whereas Psychoticism was markedly weaker on all criteria.

The reliability estimates for EPQ-RS-B Extraversion and Neuroticism fell within the range reported in English-speaking samples ($\alpha$ = .78 to .88; @eysenck1985revised; @francis1992abbreviated), Japanese ($\alpha$ = .75 to .78; @hosokawa1993reliability), Greek ($\alpha$ = .73 to .78; @alexopoulos2004psychometric), and German ($\alpha$ = .81 to .85; @francis2006german) adaptations. The Lie scale performed in the range typical of non-English versions. Psychoticism reliability in the present sample was consistent with the range of .24 to .62 that characterizes the cross-cultural literature, falling below the conventional .70 threshold regardless of which estimator was employed. This finding is not attributable to translation quality alone; it is the modal outcome for this subscale across languages and continents.

The IRT analyses added precision to this picture. The 2PL models revealed that P items had, on average, lower discrimination parameters and more extreme difficulty values than items from other subscales, resulting in a test information function that peaked at unusual theta levels and provided limited measurement precision across the practically relevant range. By contrast, E and N items showed discrimination and information patterns consistent with adequate measurement. These IRT findings converge with the classical indices and with the Italian IRT analysis by @colledani2018irt, who found that even after IRT-guided item selection, P remained the weakest EPQ-RS scale.

## Invariance
The invariance results for the EPQ-RS-B follow a pattern that is well documented in the measurement invariance literature. Full configural and metric invariance were established with ease, indicating that males and females interpreted the items in terms of the same latent constructs and that the strength of item-factor relations did not differ across groups. This is consistent with prior findings on Eysenckian measures, where the basic factor structure of extraversion, neuroticism, psychoticism, and the lie scale has been repeatedly recovered across sex and cultural groups (Barrett et al., 1998; Eysenck & Barrett, 2013; Francis et al., 1992). McLarnon and Romero (2020) similarly reported that the configural structure of shortened EPQ forms was broadly replicable across 35 countries, though they cautioned that metric and scalar equivalence required alignment corrections before cross-group comparisons could be trusted.
The localized strain observed at M10, when latent means were first constrained to equality, warrants careful interpretation. This step effectively tests whether males and females occupy the same position on each latent dimension, and its failure is not a measurement artefact but rather a substantive finding: the sexes differ in their standing on one or more of the Eysenckian traits. Such differences are among the most robustly replicated results in personality psychology. Females consistently score higher on neuroticism, while males tend to score higher on psychoticism (Alexopoulos & Kalaitzidis, 2004; Aluja et al., 2003; Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993). These mean-level differences are expected to produce misfit when latent means are forcibly equated, and this is precisely what the M10 results reflect.
The critical observation is that M13, the fully constrained model, met practical fit-change criteria. This pattern, in which intermediate mean-structure steps show strain but the terminal model is tenable, aligns with the interpretive logic articulated by Marsh et al. (2009), who argued that the most restrictive solution provides the primary evidential basis for concluding that cross-group comparisons operate within a defensible invariant framework. From this perspective, the EPQ-RS-B can be regarded as measuring the same constructs, with the same metric and scalar properties, across sexes in the present sample. This does not deny that latent mean differences exist; rather, it confirms that such differences, once incorporated into a model that also constrains all other relevant parameters, do not compromise the overall measurement framework.
It is worth noting that the recovery of fit from M10 to M13 is itself informative. The addition of residual variance and factor variance/covariance constraints in M13 evidently compensated for the strain introduced by mean constraints alone, suggesting that the misfit at M10 was partly attributable to unmodeled heterogeneity in variance parameters rather than to genuine intercept non-invariance. This interpretation is consistent with Chen's (2007) demonstration that fit-change indices are jointly sensitive to differences in means, variances, and covariances, making it hazardous to attribute misfit at any single step to a single parameter class in isolation.
The present findings should be situated alongside the well-known limitations of the psychoticism scale, which has historically exhibited low internal consistency (Eysenck & Eysenck, 1985; Alexopoulos & Kalaitzidis, 2004; Francis et al., 1992) and has been shown to contain items with gender differential item functioning (Colledani et al., 2018). Although the current invariance analysis did not isolate scale-specific sources of strain, the broader literature suggests that psychoticism items are the most likely candidates for partial non-invariance at the item level. Future studies would benefit from item-level invariance testing or alignment methods (McLarnon & Romero, 2020) to pinpoint whether the localized misfit at M10 is driven predominantly by the psychoticism or lie subscales. Despite these caveats, the overall conclusion is that the EPQ-RS-B demonstrates adequate sex measurement invariance under the 13-model framework, supporting the validity of cross-sex comparisons of Eysenckian personality dimensions in this sample.

##  Language Based Invariance
The language-invariance analyses yield a clear but qualified conclusion: the four-factor P, E, N, L structure of the EPQ-RS-B is configurally supported across groups, but the equality of factor loadings, thresholds, and higher-order parameters is not. Relative to the gender-invariance results reported earlier in which M13 met both decision rules for the EPQ-RS-B (ΔCFI = .002; ΔRMSEA = −.001), the language comparison produces a qualitatively different picture. The same instrument that behaved equivalently for men and women in this sample does not behave equivalently across the two language administrations. On its face, this pattern suggests that the constraint being tested by the language contrast is substantially more demanding than the within-language, cross-gender contrast, and that the observed item-level functioning carries language-specific variation in loadings and thresholds that cannot be fully absorbed by a common measurement model.
Three features of the result deserve emphasis rather than being smoothed over. First, the non-convergence of M5, M7, and M9 is not noise to be discarded; it signals that specific combinations of constraints, most notably those involving uniqueness equality together with intermediate mean-structure restrictions, are not jointly supported by these data. In categorical ESEM with binary items under theta parameterisation, uniqueness constraints have known identification subtleties (Bowen & Masa, 2015; Liu et al., 2017), and the failure to converge is consistent with the view that forcing equality across groups at these steps imposes more structure than the language-specific response patterns can bear. Second, the negative scaled Δχ² at M12 is not a substantive improvement in fit. It is a known behaviour of scaled-difference testing under WLSMV when the scaling factors are adjusted across nested models (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2010), and we flag it here so that subsequent readers do not over-interpret the apparent ΔCFI and ΔRMSEA gains at that step. Third, and most importantly, the ΔRMSEA-based rule indicated invariance at every converged step, while the ΔCFI-based rule did not. This divergence is by now a familiar story in the invariance literature, particularly with large samples and many items: RMSEA's parsimony correction tends to mask cross-group parameter differences that CFI detects (Chen, 2007; Khojasteh & Lo, 2015; Putnick & Bornstein, 2016). The most defensible reading of our data is the CFI-based one. Reporting only ΔRMSEA would give a misleadingly benign picture of the instrument's cross-language behaviour.
In substantive terms, the failure of metric invariance (M2) means that the factor loadings, that is, the calibrations by which items map onto their latent traits, are not equivalent across the two language groups. This is the most consequential single finding of the language-invariance analyses. When loadings differ across groups, the latent construct itself is not being indexed on a common metric, which in turn means that observed-score comparisons, effect sizes involving EPQ-RS-B scales, and correlation magnitudes with external criteria are not guaranteed to be comparable across languages (Meredith, 1993; Vandenberg & Lance, 2000). The failure of threshold invariance (M4) adds a second, compounding problem for observed-score comparisons: even if loadings were equal, unequal thresholds mean that the probability of endorsing a given item at a given level of the latent trait differs across language groups, producing systematic shifts in sum scores that are not attributable to true trait differences (Millsap, 2011). Given our earlier finding that the Psychoticism subscale is the weakest in internal consistency and IRT fit (a finding that replicates across 16+ cross-cultural EPQ-RS adaptations; see Introduction), it is plausible, although not directly tested here, that the Psychoticism and Lie items contribute disproportionately to the language non-invariance. Targeted partial-invariance or alignment analyses (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2014; Marsh et al., 2018) would be required to localise the problematic items.
The practical implication is that direct cross-language comparisons of EPQ-RS-B sum scores should be interpreted with caution. Within each language, the scales appear to measure a coherent four-factor structure; between languages, the measurement model is not strict enough to warrant treating sum-score differences, or even regression coefficients with external criteria, as unambiguously reflecting true trait differences. This is an ordinary consequence of adapting a binary, content-heterogeneous instrument across linguistic and cultural boundaries, and it is consistent with McLarnon and Romero's (2020) finding that cross-country equivalence of even shortened EPQ versions is supported only under partial-invariance frameworks or approximate-invariance methods such as alignment. Future work on the EPQ-RS-B should therefore extend beyond the exact-invariance taxonomy used here to the alignment method and item-level DIF, both of which are better suited to locating the specific items that drive non-invariance and to producing aligned scores that support latent-mean comparisons despite partial non-invariance.
Two caveats temper these conclusions. First, the taxonomy used here is the exact-invariance framework, which is known to be overly strict in many realistic cross-cultural applications (Marsh et al., 2018; van de Schoot et al., 2013). Approximate-invariance and alignment approaches often find that what appears to be a wholesale failure under exact invariance is in fact a localised failure involving a minority of items. Second, the non-convergence of three models means that the taxonomy, as applied, is incomplete. A full picture of language invariance for this instrument will require an alignment-based analysis in a follow-up study, and we plan to incorporate this as a supplementary analysis before submission.

## IRT
### Neuroticism, Extraversion, and the Lie Scale
The IRT analysis revealed a clear hierarchy of measurement quality across the four EPQ-RS-B subscales. Neuroticism performed best, with uniformly high item discriminations, well-distributed difficulty parameters, and a broad window of reliable measurement. This finding is consistent with prior IRT work on the EPQ-R Neuroticism scale. For example, Smits et al. (2021), using UK Biobank data from over 400,000 participants, reported that the 12 Neuroticism items showed generally strong discrimination, though they also noted that a reduced 7-item version was more psychometrically efficient because it eliminated items with redundant difficulty values. In the present Bangla sample, the full 12-item Neuroticism scale retained strong measurement properties, and the range of difficulty parameters was sufficiently broad to avoid the clustering problem identified by Smits et al. (2021). The marginal reliability of .83 and the empirical reliability of .80 place the Bangla Neuroticism scale comfortably within the range reported in cross-cultural adaptations, where alpha coefficients typically fall between .77 and .83 (Francis et al., 1992; Katz & Francis, 2000; Morfaki, 2021).

Extraversion and the Lie scale both achieved marginal reliabilities of .76, which are acceptable for research purposes though modest for individual-level assessment. These values are broadly comparable to the alpha coefficients reported in other non-Western adaptations. Francis et al. (2006) reported an Extraversion alpha of .85 in a German sample, and Katz and Francis (2000) reported values ranging from .78 to .87 across English-speaking countries. The somewhat lower reliability in the present sample may reflect cultural differences in the behavioral referents of Extraversion items, particularly those tapping sociability and gregariousness, which may carry different connotations in Bangladeshi collectivist cultural contexts (Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993). IRT analysis further revealed that the Lie scale contained three weak items (EYP4, EYP16, EYP45) with low discrimination, and that EYP4 was essentially inert, failing to contribute any meaningful measurement information. These findings parallel those of Colledani et al. (2019), who identified similar weak items in the Italian EPQ-R and proposed shortened versions with improved psychometric efficiency.
### The Psychoticism Problem
The most consequential finding concerns the Psychoticism subscale. The marginal reliability of .44 and the negative empirical reliability confirm that the Bangla Psychoticism scale is not functioning as a coherent measure of a single latent trait.
